Use Case Guide

Construction Software for Concrete Contractors

Concrete work moves fast and has zero tolerance for scheduling errors. Here's software that keeps your pours on track and your margins protected.

✓ Updated April 2026

Concrete contracting is one of the most operationally demanding trades. You're coordinating ready-mix deliveries with crew availability and weather windows. Miscalculate yardage and you're either short (catastrophic) or paying for unused material. Miss a pour window due to crew or equipment issues and you're rescheduling with a ready-mix plant that has a full order book. The right software makes these coordination challenges manageable.

What concrete contractors need from software

  • Accurate takeoff and estimating: Volume calculations for slabs, footings, walls, and columns — with waste factors for each pour type
  • Pour scheduling coordination: Coordinate crew, equipment, ready-mix delivery, and weather in one place
  • Job costing by pour: Actual vs. estimated yardage, labor hours, and finishing costs per job
  • Subcontractor coordination: Rebar, forming, finishing, and pump truck subs need visibility into pour schedules
  • Documentation: Mix design records, delivery tickets, temperature logs, and placement notes for QA and warranty purposes

Best software picks for concrete contractors

For concrete estimating: PlanSwift + Concrete Specific Templates

PlanSwift with concrete-specific assemblies is the most efficient estimating workflow for many concrete contractors. Linear measurements for footings, area calculations for slabs, and volume computations for walls can all be set up with waste factors, form material requirements, and labor units baked into the assemblies. The result: accurate estimates that also generate a material list for ready-mix orders.

For project management: Buildertrend

Concrete contractors working as subcontractors on residential or commercial projects benefit from Buildertrend's scheduling integration. You can see your position in the GC's project schedule, coordinate your pour dates around preceding and following trades, and communicate schedule changes without phone tag. For concrete subs working with multiple GC clients, centralizing communication through one platform reduces the coordination chaos.

Buildertrend — coordinate your pour schedule with GC timelines, manage your crew, and track job costs per project.

Try Buildertrend →

For commercial concrete: Procore or Autodesk Build

Concrete contractors doing large commercial structural work — foundations, tilt-up walls, elevated decks — often work with GCs running Procore or Autodesk Build. The most practical approach is to integrate with whatever platform your GC uses rather than running a separate system. Procore's subcontractor experience allows you to view the GC's schedule, submit daily reports, manage RFIs, and process invoices — all without paying Procore's full GC pricing.

For small concrete operations: Contractor Foreman

Small concrete contractors — flatwork specialists, residential foundations — often find Contractor Foreman's balance of features and affordability ideal. Estimates, job scheduling, crew time tracking, and basic job costing cover the core needs at $49–$249/month for unlimited users.

Pour scheduling: the critical workflow

A concrete pour has dependencies that differ from typical construction tasks:

  • Preceding work: Forming, rebar, utilities, and inspection must all be complete before pouring
  • Ready-mix coordination: Order must be placed typically 24–48 hours ahead, with truck timing coordinated to job site access
  • Crew timing: Finishers must be on site when concrete arrives — not 2 hours later
  • Weather window: Temperature, humidity, wind, and upcoming frost all affect pour timing and curing
  • Equipment availability: Pump trucks, vibrators, and finishing equipment must be confirmed before pour day

Construction software with dependency-linked scheduling and calendar integration helps manage this complexity. Set up the pour date with predecessor tasks (forming complete, inspection passed) and successor tasks (curing period, strip forms, backfill) and the schedule propagates automatically.

Job costing for concrete work

Concrete job costing tracks four cost categories: material (ready-mix yardage at actual delivered price), labor (crew hours × loaded labor rate), subcontracted work (forming, rebar, pump), and equipment (owned or rented). The key variance to watch: actual vs. estimated yardage. Estimating short means ordering more at spot price (expensive and delayed). Estimating long means paying for waste. Good job costing data from completed pours makes future estimates more accurate.

Documentation that protects you

Concrete contractors face warranty and liability claims years after project completion. Documentation that protects you:

  • Mix design records with specified strength, w/c ratio, and admixtures
  • Delivery tickets for every truck with timestamps
  • Temperature and weather records at time of pour
  • Slump test and air content results
  • Photo documentation of rebar placement, cover, and forming before and after pour
  • Curing method and duration records

Daily report software (Raken, or the daily log feature in your PM platform) is the most efficient way to capture this contemporaneously.

Bottom line

Concrete contractors need strong estimating tools (PlanSwift for volume accuracy), schedule management for pour coordination, and job costing to protect margins. The platform choice depends on project size and whether you're working as a sub (integrate with the GC's platform) or self-performing GC work (Buildertrend or Contractor Foreman at budget).

Buildertrend gives concrete GCs and subs a complete platform for scheduling, job costing, and subcontractor coordination.
